narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. Among the key elements shaping this outcome for you, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive signal, suggesting it pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, more so than for many others. In contrast, your third-year grade decile in school had a notable push in the opposite direction, making failure seem more likely because it was lower than what typically helps students succeed on the bar. Your middle family income bracket also worked in your favor to some degree, nudging the model toward seeing you as capable of passing, aligning with how a solid portion of successful students come from similar backgrounds. Compared to students who passed the bar, your LSAT score of 25 and undergrad GPA of 2.90 are below their averages of around 37 and 3.24, respectively, while your first- and third-year deciles of 2 fall well short of their typical 6 range—though these played supporting roles here. Being full-time is a common strength among those who pass, which fits your plan and didn't hurt your case. Overall, the balance tipped toward concern because your Year 3 performance outweighed the positives from your GPA and income, unlike accepted applicants whose stronger grade trajectories often secure their spots. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to 37, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Many students in similar positions have improved these areas and succeeded—reapplying after targeted preparation could shift your prediction dramatically. You're clearly driven, and with focused steps, law school remains within reach.
